---
title: "Lantana: With a Little Help of My (Claude) Friend"
subtitle: "How AI accelerated a honeypot-as-code project"
description: "How I used Claude Code to ship Lantana v1 in two months — motivation, the 2025 way, and the workflow that unlocked things."
image: og-lantana-little-help-claude.webp

tlp: clear
duration: 15
event: "Internal AI share-out"
date: 2026-07-06

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../static/styles/vigil-reveal-dark.scss]
    incremental: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    slide-number: c/t
    toc: false
    controls: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    hash-type: number
---

## Lantana: With a Little Help of My (Claude) Friend {.center}

[TLP:CLEAR]{.tlp-badge}

[INTERNAL AI SHARE-OUT · 2026-07-06]{.kicker}

How a honeypot-as-code project went from 2.5 months of Alpha to a v1 in two.

::: notes
Frame the talk: it's about workflow more than about honeypots. Lantana is the vehicle. The story is what changed when I stopped chatting with an LLM and started collaborating with an agent. 15 minutes, ~5 for Q&A, so keep it moving.
:::

## Why Lantana

[MOTIVATION]{.kicker}

- Deployed two SIEMs. Wanted to reason about the log pipeline end-to-end — without building fake sources.
- I run Adversary Engagement at work. Honeypots as data source clicked instantly.
- T-Pot is great but heavy: 20+ decoys, ELK-locked, weak on narrative.
- I wanted the opposite: few decoys, strong narratives, IaC-first, open pipeline.

::: notes
One line each. The "why" audience gets is: this wasn't a honeypot project pretending to be an AI project — it was a real thing I needed, and AI happened to change how I built it.
:::

## 84 Years Ago, in 2025

[THE PRE-MODERN WAY]{.kicker}

- Dec 2025 – Mar 2026 — Alpha built with Google Gemini Pro. Just the chat.
- Open thread. Paste context. Argue with the model like a fellow practitioner.
- Ansible playbook? Copy the answer, fix the small-but-important details.
- Context saturation → prompt rage → new thread → paste context *again*.
- The design decisions held up. The workflow felt prehistoric.

::: notes
The point of this slide: it wasn't useless — the foundation Gemini and I settled on survived every later review. It was just *stressful and slow*. Set up the contrast for what comes next.
:::

## The numbers

[BEFORE AND AFTER CLAUDE]{.kicker}

:::: columns
::: {.column width="33%"}
[9×]{style="font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 5.5em; line-height: 1; color: #4db8e0;"}

more lines written

[~3K → ~29K]{style="font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; color: #cccccc; font-size: 0.85em;"}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
[5×]{style="font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 5.5em; line-height: 1; color: #4db8e0;"}

more commits
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
[3.5×]{style="font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 5.5em; line-height: 1; color: #4db8e0;"}

more files touched
:::
::::

Same project. Same person. Not full-time. 2.5 months before Claude → 2 months after.

::: notes
Exact figures if pressed: 46 → 234 commits (215 substantive + 19 dependency bumps). 65 → 226 files. +3,180 → +29,204 lines. Claude onboarded 2026-04-26 (commit f6f69f5). Last commit in the window: 2026-06-24. Don't read the numbers aloud — let the ratios land.
:::

## 6 hours → 5 minutes

[THE INSIGHT]{.kicker}

Enrichment was thrashing exhausted API rate limits.

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
> "…is it feasible and easy to create a check like this: if the X previous enrichment tries for that source failed due to rate_limit, skip the rest? it'd save much much time."
:::

- Claude: ~20 lines, one function, plus tests. Implemented in ~10 minutes.
- Result: ~99% drop. Full-day enrichment now runs in under five minutes.

::: notes
This is the archetype of the collaboration for me. I saw the *shape* of the problem — a threat detection engineer notices API rate-limit thrash the way a mechanic hears a bad bearing. Claude implemented a per-provider consecutive-failure counter, with sensible thresholds, faster than I could have looked up the tenacity docs. Neither alone would have done this in an afternoon. Together, 10 minutes.
:::

## Caveats & Takeaways

[WHAT I LEARNED]{.kicker}

- **Configure the AI** — keep `CLAUDE.md` lean. Bloated context makes agents *worse* (ETH Zurich).
- **Prompt properly** — plan mode first. Let Claude *interview you* before it writes code (Cherny).
- **Manage context** — `/clear` between tasks beats `/compact`. Context rot is real.
- **Let it verify itself** — TDD-style loops. And let it commit, on git-tracked projects.
- Try old ideas every three months. The models keep getting better.

::: notes
The "let it commit" bullet has a story behind it — an experimental repo, no git yet, I was still committing myself, and a resort operation wiped a data file. Claude recovered it from its own session JSONL, which was lucky. That's when I decided: let Claude commit, with the right instructions in the user CLAUDE.md. If someone asks about the recovery story in Q&A, tell it then — otherwise skip to save time.
:::

## Alone we're both weaker

[TOGETHER]{.kicker}

- **Lantana** — `github.com/lopes/lantana`
- **Posts** — `lopes.id/log/lantana-1-honeypot-as-code` · `lantana-2-data-pipeline`
- **My CLAUDE.md** — `github.com/lopes/dotfiles/.../CLAUDE.md`
- **Track — AI Literacy** — Yegge, Cherny, Chase H, Miessler

Questions?

::: notes
Land the frame one more time: neither Claude nor I would have shipped this in two months alone. The AI didn't replace me — it made me faster where I was slow, and stayed out of the way where I was already fast. Then open the floor.
:::
